why: why validate forms on the client side
why is server side validation still needed
how: how to validate forms in javascript
output: custom validity message appears on submit

1_beginner_explanation.summary: Form Validation is the process of checking a user's input to make sure it is correct BEFORE sending it to the server. For example, making sure an email actually has an '@' symbol, or ensuring a password is long enough. You can do this using built-in HTML attributes (`required`, `minlength`), or by writing custom JavaScript logic.
1_beginner_explanation.analogy: Validation is the bouncer at the door of a club. If you don't meet the dress code (like forgetting your email address), the bouncer (JavaScript) stops you from entering (submitting the form) and tells you to go fix it.

2_internal_mechanism.execution_flow: When a form is submitted, the browser first runs its internal HTML5 validation algorithm. If any input fails (e.g., it is `required` but empty), the browser fires an `invalid` event and stops the submission automatically. If you prevent default, you can run custom JS regex checks.
2_internal_mechanism.memory: Validation logic does not consume significant memory, but complex Regular Expressions can consume significant CPU cycles (Regex catastrophic backtracking).

3_real_world_example.use_case: Checking if passwords match before allowing the user to register.

4_common_confusion.trap: Thinking that Client-Side Validation (JavaScript) makes your app secure. It does NOT. A malicious user can simply disable JavaScript in their browser, bypass all your rules, and send garbage data directly to your server.
4_common_confusion.fix: Client-Side Validation is ONLY for a good User Experience (UX) (fast error messages). Security ALWAYS requires Server-Side Validation.

6_edge_case.quirk: You can hook into the browser's native HTML5 validation UI using the Constraint Validation API (`input.setCustomValidity()`) to show standard browser error popups with your own custom messages.
